segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber' title card, accompanied by a deep male voice announcing, "On Prime Crime." The scene transitions to a desert-style home with a stone facade and garage, as a distressed male voice on a 911 call states, "I just had a 12-year-old boy show up here at my front door asking for help. We need the cops here as soon as possible." This is followed by a dark, nighttime scene with flashing police lights, while a narrator's voice declares, "It's the 911 call heard around the world," casting the spotlight on a "popular YouTuber." A montage then shows a woman with blonde hair in various domestic and social settings—shopping with children, holding jars of peanut butter, posing with another woman in festive holiday hats, and speaking at a professional event. During this montage, a distinct female voice offers commentary, stating, "If you're in a position of power and you turn your power and give it over to the kids, that is control." The narrator then adds, "And her partner in crime," as a different female voice identifies herself, saying, "Hi, I'm Jody Hilderbrand. I am at the second annual Women's Retreat." Another male voice expresses shock, stating, "I'm as shocked as anybody. Her and Ruby were just the two wrong things to come together." The narrator sets the stage for a complex narrative, saying, "In a story that has layers..." The video dramatically shifts to real bodycam footage, displaying a timestamp and device ID, showing a man in a white shirt and red shorts standing on a porch. An officer's voice is heard saying, "She can stay right here on the porch so you can see her," followed by "What are you afraid of, sir?" The narrator concludes, "...upon layers." The scene cuts to the woman with blonde hair, now in a striped prison uniform, being escorted by a law enforcement officer in a courtroom. This is followed by a woman in a blue hoodie cooking in a modern kitchen, stirring a pot with a wooden spoon. Throughout these visuals, a female voice describes Jody, saying, "Jody is very smart in how she approaches her therapeutic modalities. It's like a frog being boiled in water. You know, you start off with the cold water and slowly turn up the temperature." The segment concludes with a close-up of bubbling liquid in a pot, possibly a stew or soup, before returning to the 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME' title card, as background music swells.

Jesse Weber, a man in a sharp navy blue suit and white shirt, then appears in a modern studio with brick walls, a spiral staircase, and a courthouse graphic on a screen. He speaks directly to the camera, gesturing expressively as he introduces himself and the show, stating, "Hey there everybody, I'm Jesse Weber and welcome to Prime Crime. This is where we break down the most compelling true crime cases in America." He sets up the next story, which "had the entire nation buzzing" and "all began with one chilling 911 phone call." The video then cuts to an aerial view of a remote desert landscape with a modern house nestled among rocky terrain, as a male voice from a 911 call is heard saying, "I just had a 12-year-old boy show up here at my front door asking for help." A narrator (Jesse Weber) then specifies the context: "August 30th, 2023, Ivins, Utah. What you're hearing is not your typical 911 phone call, but this isn't your typical case. And it all begins with one woman." The scene shifts to a close-up of a woman with blonde hair, first in a floral top and maroon cardigan, speaking earnestly in a brightly lit room with a colorful geometric background, stating, "Worshipping your child will be placing your child's desires over responsibility." This is followed by a different shot of the same woman, now in a black-and-white checkered shirt, smiling broadly with her hands raised in a shrugging gesture. The narrator (Jesse Weber) identifies her as "Ruby" as the segment concludes with a yellow and black 'Morgan & Morgan' sponsorship tag displayed in the corner of the desert footage.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Ruby Frankie, a woman with blonde hair in a floral top, speaking animatedly about being a 'crazy mom' who loves dressing her kids in red, white, and blue, a tradition since they were babies. The blonde woman, now in a black-and-white checkered shirt, smiles and shrugs, commenting that the kids are more excited about the presents their siblings are getting. UT Criminal Defense Attorney Jeremy Deus then explains that 'Eight Passengers' was started by Ruby Frankie and her husband Kevin several years ago. An animated drawing of a van filled with stick-figure passengers visually represents the channel. A man and woman on a couch smile with expressive gestures, followed by a group photo of the large Frankie family in winter clothing, including Kevin, Julie, Shari, Chad, Ruby, Russell, Abby, and Eve. An excited female voice, likely Ruby Frankie, exclaims, 'You guys, we are in Florida!', with a male voice adding that 'the kids had no idea,' as a woman with a blue backpack and a child with a red backpack board a white van. The segment concludes with Jeremy Deus describing how Ruby and Kevin have six children together, and their YouTube channel was an initial mommy blog inviting people into their family life, as a group of children walk down a residential sidewalk with a man, heading towards a house.

This cuts to a dimly lit living room where several children lounge on a couch under blankets, as Shari Franke's voice continues, adding that "my neighbors have been telling me that they have been left home alone." segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a large, two-story beige house under a bright blue sky, as a male narrator explains that Russell and his sister Eve were taken to the hospital for malnutrition and injuries, prompting the question: "Where are Ruby's other minor children?" The narrator details that officers from Springville Police and Utah County Major Crimes Task Force went to "Ruby Franke's home in Springville" to locate "two outstanding children that were under the age of 18." A family photo of two smiling young girls and other family members briefly appears before the bodycam returns, showing multiple officers in tactical gear approaching and then kicking in the front door, as the narrator confirms they "kick the door down" and "clear the house." Another bodycam segment shows an officer walking toward a different house, with on-screen subtitles indicating a search for a minor sibling and a DCFS warrant for custody. Distorted audio, likely from Det. Mulford, confirms, "There's one other sibling that we're still looking for, considered still in dangerous and DCFS has a warrant to take them both into custody." The segment concludes with a photo of two women laughing in a car, as subtitles identify Det. Mulford and state "Pam was seen picking up a minor from a rec center," further supported by the distorted audio stating, "Pam was seen picking up [inaudible] from the Springville Rec Center around 1:15."

Officer Hawkins, in a dark uniform with visible tattoos, asks a man identified as Roy, in a white t-shirt and red shorts at a stone-walled doorway, “Is Pam in today?” Roy identifies her as his wife. Back at the residential porch, the officer, with a yellow tool on his belt, tells Pam and Roy, “We'll go ahead and get the copy of the warrant for you, okay?” Pam, appearing cautious, insists, “You have a warrant for my house?” The officer confirms it is “specifically for you... for you and the child.” Roy asks, “What is going on?” The officer then reaches out to take Pam’s hand, and she pulls back slightly. The scene briefly cuts to Brian Schnee, a KUTV2 News Anchor/Reporter in a gray suit, speaking directly to the camera in a studio with a red backdrop, before returning to the doorway as his narration explains that police “place her into custody... putting cuffs on her and putting her in one of the patrol cars.”

She appears distressed, with hands clasped and legs drawn up, as a female officer's voice asks, "Do you need medical attention at all?" and "Is there a reason you're so scared right now?" Pam quietly responds, "It's us." The officer continues with a reassuring tone, "My intention is not to scare you," and asks, "Are you okay? Or is there something that I can do to make you not feel as scared right now?" The footage is overlaid with timestamps and 'AXON BODY 3' identifiers. The segment concludes with a still photo of a smiling blonde woman with sunglasses surrounded by four children, while the narrator adds, "The children don't really want to engage with police. They're standoffish," suggesting a family context to the unfolding situation.

The camera, likely mounted on an officer’s body, captures Roy’s gestures and the porch setting, which includes a black door with a yellow wreath, stone walls, and a rocking chair. The scene then shifts to a woman, identified as Pam, sitting on a rocking chair on the same porch, her face blurred for privacy. She appears distressed, with her hands clasped and legs drawn up, as the officer asks if she needs medical attention or if she’s scared. The officer continues to ask if there’s something he can do to help her feel less scared. The footage is overlaid with timestamps and 'AXON BODY 3' identifiers, confirming it’s real-time police recording. The segment ends with a still photo of a smiling woman with blonde hair and sunglasses, surrounded by four children, suggesting a family context to the unfolding situation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with real bodycam footage from August 30, 2023, showing Officer Hawkins, in a dark police uniform with a badge, heard explaining to a DCFS staffer beside a black pickup truck that the "girls have been melancholy... they're just sitting on the porch and really said no word to us. They whisper stuff to each other." The DCFS staffer, with a tattooed arm, is heard responding that the "older sister was saying that it might be rough." A male narrator's voice then states, "The children are ultimately taken into state custody. Ruby Frankie and Jody Hildebrant are arrested and charged with six counts of aggravated child abuse each with respect to Russell and Eve's injuries." This narration plays over a montage of personal photos: two women smiling in holiday hats in a desert, a young girl riding on a boy’s shoulders, a man in a checkered shirt speaking in an office, two women smiling in an office setting, a young girl with a blue balloon, and children playing in a backyard. The scene transitions to a YouTube playlist titled 'YouTube Mom Child Abuse Case – UT v. Ruby Franke'. The man in the checkered shirt, previously seen in the montage, is then heard discussing the legal aspects of the case, stating, "You're talking about serious bodily injury and you get to the aggravated child abuse portion of it, which is a second-degree felony. That's a one to 15-year sentence, which is what these charges ended up being. Sometimes that's related to emotional abuse. Sometimes that's related to severe malnutrition. It can be a combination of them." The male narrator's voice returns, declaring, "The arrest of Frankie and Hildebrant sets off a firestorm." Finally, Jesse Weber, in a gray suit, is seen and heard speaking directly to the camera with expressive gestures, adding, "It really gained the interest from people around the world about how this could take place and how these two lives..." The segment concludes with a photo of two women laughing in a car, one wearing sunglasses, with a 'COMING UP' banner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a 'COMING UP' teaser for 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber,' as a male voice, later identified as Jesse Weber, dramatically states, "the story, when we return, Ruby Frankie makes a decision that upends the case, and secrets are revealed about what actually happened to the Frankie children," accompanied by suspenseful music. Visually, a woman in a winter hat and coat is shown, followed by a family scene in a modern kitchen where a blonde woman interacts with two children. The scene then transitions to a legal document highlighting phrases like 'physical torture' and 'denied adequate water,' which the audio implies are details from the "Ruby Frankie story." Another male voice then shares, "I think I share the same reaction that a lot of people share. The show's title card, 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME with Jesse Weber,' appears. Jesse Weber then appears in a studio, wearing headphones and speaking into a Shure microphone against a rusted metal backdrop, gesturing expressively. He addresses the audience, stating, "Hey, so we're going to get back to Prime Crime in just a minute, but what is the Ruby Frankie story make very clear? It's that you have to know who you're surrounding yourself with, right?" He transitions to a sponsor segment, introducing "truthfinder.com" as a service that can provide "actual safety." A lower-third graphic displays the sponsor URL 'www.truthfinder.com/LCPrimeCrime.' The video then shows a 'People Search Service' webpage with fields for 'First Name' and 'Last Name,' as Jesse Weber explains Truthfinder is "one of the largest public record search services in the entire world" that helps people "learn the facts about the people in their lives." A close-up of a hand using a smartphone to access the Truthfinder app, typing 'John' and 'Smith' into the search fields, illustrates his explanation of how to "type in a name" on their website. The URL 'www.truthfinder.com/LCPrimeCrime' remains visible.

segments.integrated_caption: Against a backdrop of dramatic, investigative music, a male voiceover introduces accounts of child abuse against Ruby Franke's own children. The scene then shifts to a woman with blonde hair, wearing a black top with a white collar, speaking directly to the camera in a bright, modern home, discussing "empowering your children through setting boundaries, teaching them responsibility, and helping them to heal from anxiety," inviting viewers to "Hope to see you there." On-screen text highlights these themes. The male voiceover returns, noting "Frankie's admission of guilt and willingness to testify against her co-defendant, mental health counselor Jody Hilderbrant," questioning if Hilderbrant could fight her child abuse charges. Visually, Ruby Franke is seen in a gray and white striped top, seated in a courtroom with law enforcement officers nearby, alongside a 'truthfinder' logo. A quick montage follows, showing a joyful family moment, Ruby Franke's mugshot over a prison fence, and a woman in a white jacket and red dress lounging. Jeremy Deus, a UT Criminal Defense Attorney, then speaks from an office, explaining legal concepts like a "possible defense of appropriate parenting" and the distinction between intentional and reckless actions concerning "serious bodily injuries," framing it as a "defense about level of punishment." The video briefly shows children playing outdoors with money and drawing, then indoors with toys, followed by a still photo of a smiling woman with blonde hair in a gray cardigan. The scene returns to a courtroom from December 27, 2023, where Ruby Franke stands beside Judge John Walton, identified by on-screen text, with a law enforcement officer nearby. The male voiceover concludes that "just like Ruby Frankie, Jody Hilderbrant decides not to put up a fight," as Judge Walton is heard directly asking, "Miss Hilderbrant, how do you plead to count one?"

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on December 27, 2023, in a formal courtroom, showing defendant Jodi Hildebrandt in a gray and white striped prison uniform, standing with her attorney and a law enforcement officer. A male voice details charges of "aggravated child abuse, a second-degree felony," to which Jodi Hildebrandt's voice repeatedly responds "Guilty" for counts three, five, and six. Interspersed are personal photos: two women in holiday hats in a desert, a woman in a red coat with an American flag, a woman in a floral dress holding a young boy, and a woman in a home office with headphones. A legal document then appears, detailing Jodi's guilty plea for aggravated child abuse, specifically highlighting that she forced a child to jump into a cactus multiple times. A male narrator explains that Jodi's case became more difficult after Ruby (unseen) pleaded guilty and was to testify, as some of the charged conduct was what Ruby was doing to the children. Another male voice confirms Jodi's plea agreement admits to child abuse, adding the disturbing detail from the plea form that she made a child jump into a cactus multiple times. The segment concludes with an exterior shot of a courthouse, a woman in a gray hoodie smiling at a microphone, and a young girl in a blue dress smiling near a sink.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen courtroom shot showing two women in gray and white striped prison uniforms, suggesting they are defendants, as a narrator states that in "2024, the duo were officially sentenced," adding that "Frankie was up first." The scene then transitions to a courtroom on February 20, 2024, where Washington County Attorney Eric Clarke, wearing a dark blue suit, stands at a podium with two law enforcement officers in beige uniforms behind him. Clarke's voice is heard detailing how Ruby Franke and her business partner committed "horrible acts of child abuse" from May to August 2023, holding two children, aged 9 and 11, in a "concentration camp-like setting," and that "heaven only knows how much longer" the older child could have survived had they not run away. An aerial view of a modern, gray house in a remote desert landscape with red rock formations and sparse vegetation then appears, followed by a still photo of a woman in an orange fleece jacket and wide-brimmed hat laughing with a young boy in a red-and-blue plaid shirt on a wooden porch, depicting a joyful family moment. The video returns to the courtroom, showing Ruby Franke, the defendant, in her prison uniform, standing beside her attorney, a man with gray hair and glasses in a black suit. Franke's voice then takes over, stating, "For the past four years, I've chosen to follow counsel and guidance that has led me into a dark delusion," explaining how her "distorted version of reality went largely unchecked" as she isolated herself from anyone who challenged her, believing the world was an "evil place" filled with "cops who control, hospitals that injure, government agencies that brainwash, husbands who refuse to protect, and children who need..." before her speech is cut off. The final scene cuts to a domestic setting where a woman with blonde hair in a gray sweater is seated at a table with a young girl in a green-and-white striped shirt eating a pastry, while a young boy in a black-and-white patterned shirt looks on from behind, creating a warm, familial atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a cheerful domestic scene, showing a bald man and a woman with blonde hair, both in matching blue shirts, laughing and stretching in bed under white covers. This joyful bedroom scene quickly shifts to an outdoor suburban setting where the bald man, now in a black jacket, stands beside a white van and a U-Haul truck, gesturing dramatically as the woman, in a blue jacket, steps out of the van. On-screen text identifies her as Ruby, crying and apologizing. Over a somber, emotional piano track, Ruby's tearful voice is heard, stating she was "abused" and addressing "Kevin, my husband of more than 23 years," expressing, "You are the love of my life. I am so sorry to leave to you to finish what we both started together." The scene then cuts to Ruby, now in a maroon top, hugging a young girl and a young boy, speaking directly to the camera with tearful sincerity. Her voice continues, addressing "my babies," explaining she was "so disoriented that I believed dark was light and light was wrong," and affirming, "I would do anything in this world for you." The tone becomes more formal as the scene transitions to a courtroom, where Ruby, in a gray and white striped prison uniform, stands beside her attorney. Her voice continues, expressing her commitment "to continuing my learning until all of my toxic layers are shed and I am ready to re-enter as a contributing member of our beautiful society." On-screen text reiterates this commitment to personal growth and reintegration. The camera then focuses on Judge John Walton, seated at the bench in his black robe, reviewing documents, as his voice delivers the sentence: "that Ms. Frankie serve four counts, four 1 to 15 year sentences based on her convictions for four counts of aggravated child abuse. They will serve consecutively." The final shot is a wide view of the solemn courtroom, showing the judge, Ruby and her attorney, law enforcement officers, and spectators, including a young boy in the audience.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Jodi Hildebrandt, a blonde woman smiling in a blue shirt in a casual indoor setting, as a male narrator states, "And then, it was Jodi Hildebrandt's turn." The scene quickly shifts to a formal courtroom, where Jodi, now in a gray and white striped prison uniform, walks beside her attorney, a man in a gray suit, under the 'LAW & CRIME PRIME CRIME' banner identifying her as 'JODI HILDEBRANDT DEFENDANT'. Intimate home footage then shows a young girl in a pink shirt and a young boy in a green and white striped shirt, both receiving a gentle touch on the chin from an adult hand. Back in the courtroom, Jodi is seen in profile, looking down somberly, her voice now heard, emotionally stating, "I sincerely love these children. I desire for them to heal physically and emotionally." She continues, explaining her decision not to go to trial to prevent them from emotionally reliving the experience, which she believed would be "detrimental to them," concluding, "My hope and prayer is that they will heal and move forward to have beautiful lives." The video briefly shows Jodi smiling in a pink jacket, holding up a 'Jodi Hildebrandt ConneXions Masterclass' notebook. The male narrator then interjects, "But not everyone was buying what Jodi was selling," as Jessi Hildebrandt, Jodi’s niece, with short platinum blonde hair and a neck tattoo, appears against a blue backdrop. Jessi speaks directly to the camera with a clear, critical tone, expressing her difficulty coming to terms with Jodi being held accountable. She reveals she's been trying to talk about this for "almost 15 years" and that "people that needed to hear didn't listen," asserting, "Nothing was done. It's so obviously a manipulation tactic to save face. She knows exactly what she's doing." The segment concludes with Jodi back in the solemn, wood-paneled courtroom, seated beside her attorney, then standing at the defendant’s table addressing the court, with law enforcement and a Utah state flag visible in the background, all set to a serious background music track.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a courtroom where Jodi Hildebrandt, in a gray and white striped prison uniform, stands with her attorney, facing forward as a formal voice declares that those with specialized training "are supposed to protect children," but "you didn't do that," instead, "you terrorized children." A narrator then states that "Like Ruby Frankie, the judge sentenced Jodi Hildebrandt to four consecutive sentences," as the visuals transition to split-screen mugshots of Ruby Franke and Jodi Hildebrandt, followed by a personal photo of them smiling together. Jodi Hildebrandt is then seen in a light blue hoodie, speaking directly to the camera in a modern kitchen, gesturing. Subsequently, UT Criminal Defense Attorney Jeremy Deus appears in an office, explaining that for "second-degree felonies," while the top end is "30 years," they "are not going to do 30 years" due to a sentencing matrix, likely facing "18 months" as a minimum. He elaborates on the board of pardons and parole's role in deciding their parole at the 18-month mark or if "they need more time in prison," considering factors like treatment, demeanor, and the children's wishes, explicitly naming "Ruby and Jodi" as he discusses their situation. The video concludes with exterior shots of a Washington County Sheriff’s transport bus driving past a high-security facility with chain-link fencing and razor wire in a desert landscape, emphasizing the institutional environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a white Washington County Sheriff’s transport bus driving along a paved road beside a high-security facility enclosed by chain-link fencing topped with razor wire, establishing a somber, institutional atmosphere, as a male voice states a minimum sentence of "39.6 months." The scene transitions to an outdoor interview with Eric Clarke, Washington County Attorney, dressed in a dark suit and tie, holding a black umbrella under overcast skies, who declares this is "one of the worst child abuse cases we've seen," deeming a four-year minimum for "Ruby's actions" "entirely appropriate." He expresses hope that "Jodi serves more time than that" and isn't released "until everybody is completely confident that she's no longer a risk." Visually, Jodi Hildebrandt, in a gray and white striped prison uniform, is then escorted by two sheriff’s deputies, one with 'SHERIFF' clearly visible on his vest, into a courtroom. A still image of two women laughing in a car, overlaid on barbed wire, contrasts past joy with current legal proceedings. A narrator's voice then explains that "with Hildebrandt and Frankie behind bars," "Kevin Frankie has officially filed for divorce from Ruby" and is "actively fighting to regain custody of his young children." This narrative accompanies a domestic kitchen scene where a bald man in a dark hoodie prepares food, followed by a joyful car ride with children dancing and clapping in the backseat. Randy Kester, Kevin Franke’s attorney, appears in a video call from an office, wearing a blue checkered shirt, stating that once Kevin "found out the full truth about how these children had been treated while they were in Ruby's care" and "some other things that have been going on behind the scenes," he "just thought it was the right time to do this" in terms of "getting the kids back," describing a court process to help them "become rehabilitated." The segment concludes with a warm family gathering in a living room, showing the bald man reading a book to children, another child running past, and a young girl in a pink shirt being gently touched on the chin by an adult hand, conveying familial connection amidst the legal discussions.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a cozy domestic scene, showing a young girl in pink pajamas reading a book on a bed with a checkered blanket, alongside a man in a striped shirt also reading. An unidentified male voice speaks gravely about the "trauma they've been through with Ruby and Jodi" and the need to "work them through that," also mentioning "Kevin." The scene transitions to a kitchen where a young girl in a pink shirt and another in a patterned top eat snacks at a table with a bald man in a black shirt, who appears to be interacting with them. Jessi Hildebrandt, a woman with short platinum blonde hair and a neck tattoo, then appears speaking directly to the camera against a blue background, her voice becoming the dominant narrative. She speaks emphatically about "narcissistic or psychopathic abuse," stating she "can't explain it" and that "children" lack the "biological mental facilities to understand what's going on." The visual cuts to a young girl in a white dress carrying sandwiches to a group of children sharing them on an orange polka-dotted rug. A still photo of a woman with long blonde hair and three children smiling in a tropical setting is shown, as Jessi's voice continues, asserting that "this is not going away" and "these children are going to deal with this potentially for the rest of their lives." She adds, "This nearly killed me," urging viewers to "trust your instincts" and "build up that muscle of intuition" because "the things that you are feeling are true." The video then shows a young girl in a striped shirt hugging a woman in a checkered shirt who holds a baby in a tiger-striped onesie. Jessi's voice concludes, emphasizing to "trust yourself" so that "people like Jodi and Frankie are not held in positions of power," adding they "can be your neighbors, they can be your aunts, they can be your mothers," as the final segment features a split-screen interview with two women, one in a floral shirt and the other in a purple top, both speaking seriously.
